what_it_does: Acceptance test for FsStore's per-ref version-history index (L6). It verifies that set_ref appends a (timestamp, CAS) entry, ref_history returns ordered entries, head remains the live current value, prior CAS bytes remain fetchable, atomic overwrite and persistence are preserved, empty histories are honest, histories are isolated per ref, reads can be bounded, identical consecutive CAS values are recorded as separate writes, and concurrent set_ref appends produce an exact, parseable history count.
observations: Imports FsStore from store.fs_store and uses a temporary /tmp/company-version-history-store directory.
Defines a manual _check helper that prints PASS/FAIL and returns the condition for accumulation.
Runs ten numbered checks ranging from basic history ordering to an 8-thread/500-write concurrency storm.
Expects ref_history to parse every line after concurrent writes and to contain exactly N_THREADS * M_WRITES entries.
Checks that no .tmp files remain in the refs directory after atomic replace.
Resets the store directory mid-test with shutil.rmtree before the concurrency section.
Uses plain threading and equality assertions rather than pytest/unittest.
Tests that re-writing the same CAS appends another history entry rather than deduplicating.
standouts: Concurrency storm asserting exact line count and parseability of the version-history file after thousands of unsynchronized appends.
Explicit preservation contract: head live, atomic os.replace, CAS survival, and lineage untouched.
Decision to version all refs and treat identical consecutive CAS values as legitimate writes is encoded in assertions.
conventions: Top-of-file module docstring states acceptance level, section reference, run command, and design decisions.
Manual sys.path insertion to import project modules.
Inline print-based test reporting with PASS/FAIL labels.
Numbered acceptance checks mapping directly to requirements.
Hard reset of the store directory between test phases.
concerns: Concurrency test writes to a shared store instance without explicit test-level synchronization; relies on FsStore implementation for thread safety.
Mid-test shutil.rmtree and store re-instantiation mutates global state and could hide cross-phase contamination.
Uses /tmp path which may be shared or cleaned by the OS, though the test recreates it.
No cleanup of the store directory at exit.
Thread storm uses the same CAS repeatedly, so it does not exercise distinct values under concurrency.
No exception handling around the storm; a torn line would raise and fail the whole run.
notes: A runnable acceptance contract that treats the version-history append as a preserve-critical hot path and proves it under a threaded write storm.
questions: What is the exact on-disk format of the per-ref history index (file path, JSON schema, line-delimited)?
How does FsStore serialize concurrent appends (file locking, atomic rename, append-only)?
What is the resolution and source of the ts field (monotonic clock, wall clock, integer/float)?
Does ref_history limit apply before or after parsing all lines?
Is there a maximum history file size or truncation policy?
What does lineage() do and how is it kept untouched?
What is the relationship between this test and other L-level acceptance tests?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to acceptance-test L6 version-history behavior, and the file exactly implements that: it exercises set_ref appending, ref_history reading, head preservation, CAS survival, bounded reads, persistence, and concurrency. Matches.
apparent_themes: live current ref vs temporal provenance
intention_signals: The file is trying to prove that adding a per-ref version-history index does not break existing live-ref semantics and that concurrent set_ref appends remain safe and exact.
novelty: The explicit concurrency storm test that treats the version-history append as a hot-path preserve-critical behavior and asserts exact line count plus parseability after thousands of unsynchronized appends.
contribution: A runnable acceptance contract enumerating expected L6 version-history behaviors, including preservation of head/overwrite/CAS semantics, per-ref isolation, bounded history reads, persistence across store reloads, and a stress test for concurrent append correctness.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance test for an FsStore per-ref version-history index verifying ordered append-on-set_ref, live head preservation, CAS byte survival, bounded reads, persistence, and concurrent append correctness.
intention_for_embedding: To validate that adding a cheap append-only per-ref version-history index preserves existing ref semantics and remains correct under concurrent writes.

symbols.does: Prints a PASS/FAIL line for a labeled boolean condition and returns the condition.
symbols.description: Small test helper used throughout main to report individual acceptance checks; it returns cond so the caller can accumulate an overall ok flag.

symbols.does: Runs the full L6 version-history acceptance suite against FsStore.
symbols.description: Sets up a temporary store, executes ten numbered checks covering history ordering, head preservation, CAS survival, atomic overwrite, empty histories, per-ref isolation, bounded reads, reload persistence, duplicate CAS writes, and a threaded concurrency storm, then prints overall pass/fail.

symbols.does: Worker that repeatedly calls set_ref on the same logical address with the same CAS.
symbols.description: Nested function used in the concurrency test; each invocation performs M_WRITES set_ref calls to LOGICAL with cas_x, intended to stress the atomic append path of the version-history index.
